titleStmt.title: Letter from Margaret May Loughren to Augustine Birrell, 26 March 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Margaret May Loughren

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Margaret May Loughren (b. 1889) to the Chief Crown Secretary Augustine Birrell (1850-1933). Margaret is a native of Co. Roscommon but writing from America. Margaret writes to Birrell in light of her brother's enlistment in the British Army. John J. Loughren (b. 1900) enlisted a month before the letter was sent and was only sixteen. Margaret pleads for Birrell to send him home as she says the army 'does not need the boy as much as he needs his home.'Birrell served as the chief secretary of Ireland from 1907 to 1916. Birrell was a liberal politician praised for his reforms of the Irish education system. The Easter Rising caught him unawares and, occupied in London, there was little he could do. A later inquiry into the Rising held Birrell chiefly responsible for the events that occurred. Birrell offered his resignation on 1 May 1916.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers, CSO RP/1916/6833/1

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
U.S
This is a War Office matter
but perhaps the Police can say in
what Rgt. Longhren enlisted
A.P.M.
15/4/16 I G.
Please ascertain.
17/4 Lennox Dale, Mass. U.S.A. March 26th 1916 I do not know how to
address you so shall not
make a mistake. My brother
John J. Loughren of
Kilglass Strokestown Co
Roscommon enlisted about
a month ago and as he
was only seventeen last
October 27th I thought I
would let you know the
facts as surely his
country does not need
that boy as much as he
needs his home, he did
not have a mother since
he was 9 and poor chap
and I'm sure he can
be of no use to the Gov-
ernment. I am sure you
do not know the cir-
cumstances and neither
does the Government as
it surely must be un-
lawful to take a boy
scarcely seventeen. I beg
of you to let him go
home to his father
as he is alone but for
him. I know England
does not want her children
so bad as that. Give
him please your attention
as I am sure he needs
it forthough his spirit
was willing to do his best
for his Country by this
time his flesh must have
weakened. If you do this
as I know you will
for me I shall be glad
to pay what it may cost
and remember I shall
always pray for those who
helped me. I feel responsible
for him as three years
he begged to come here
and I would not take
him. I remain Sincerely
a British Subject Margaret May
Loughren
Chief Sec. Birrell of Ireland Office of the Chief Sec. Dublin Ireland
Back of envelope. No text.
